(French pronunciation: [[sɛ̃ ʒɑ̃ syʁ ʁiʃ(ə)ljø]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"), lit. 'Saint-Jean on [Richelieu](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River")') is a city in eastern [Montérégie](/wiki/Mont%C3%A9r%C3%A9gie "Montérégie") in the [Canadian province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), about 40 kilometres (25 mi) southeast of [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), roughly halfway between Montreal and the [Canada–United States border](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border") with the state of [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"). It straddles the west and east banks of the [Richelieu River](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River") (which is accompanied by a canal) at the northernmost navigable point of [Lake Champlain](/wiki/Lake_Champlain "Lake Champlain"). As of December 2019, the population was 98,036. The city has experienced steady growth, up from the official 2021 Census count of 97,873 residents. It hosts the annual [International Balloon Festival of Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu](/wiki/International_Balloon_Festival_of_Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu "International Balloon Festival of Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu"), a [hot air balloon festival](/wiki/Hot_air_balloon_festival "Hot air balloon festival") which attracts thousands of tourists.

**Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu** (French pronunciation: [[sɛ̃ ʒɑ̃ syʁ ʁiʃ(ə)ljø]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"), lit. 'Saint-Jean on [Richelieu](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River")') is a city in eastern [Montérégie](/wiki/Mont%C3%A9r%C3%A9gie "Montérégie") in the [Canadian province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), about 40 kilometres (25 mi) southeast of [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), roughly halfway between Montreal and the [Canada–United States border](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border") with the state of [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"). It straddles the west and east banks of the [Richelieu River](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River") (which is accompanied by a canal) at the northernmost navigable point of [Lake Champlain](/wiki/Lake_Champlain "Lake Champlain"). As of December 2019, the population was 98,036. The city has experienced steady growth, up from the official 2021 Census count of 97,873 residents. It hosts the annual [International Balloon Festival of Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu](/wiki/International_Balloon_Festival_of_Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu "International Balloon Festival of Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu"), a [hot air balloon festival](/wiki/Hot_air_balloon_festival "Hot air balloon festival") which attracts thousands of tourists.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [21st-century municipal history of Quebec](/wiki/21st-century_municipal_history_of_Quebec "21st-century municipal history of Quebec")

Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu was founded upon the establishment of [Fort Saint-Jean](/wiki/Fort_Saint-Jean_(Quebec) "Fort Saint-Jean (Quebec)") in 1666 by soldiers of the French Carignan-Salières Regiment. Permanent settlement began in the 1780s after the American Revolution, when French-Canadian settlers and British Loyalists were drawn to the site of the former garrison.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Historically, the city has been an important transportation hub. The [first railway line in British North America](/wiki/Champlain_and_St._Lawrence_Railroad "Champlain and St. Lawrence Railroad") connected it with [La Prairie](/wiki/La_Prairie,_Quebec "La Prairie, Quebec") in 1836. It hosts the annual [International Balloon Festival of Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu](/wiki/International_Balloon_Festival_of_Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu "International Balloon Festival of Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu"), a [hot air balloon festival](/wiki/Hot_air_balloon_festival "Hot air balloon festival") which attracts thousands of tourists who come to see the hundreds of balloons in the sky each August.

The [Chambly Canal](/wiki/Chambly_Canal "Chambly Canal") extends 20 kilometres (12 mi) north along the west bank of the river and provides modern freight passage to [Chambly](/wiki/Chambly,_Quebec "Chambly, Quebec") and the [St. Lawrence River](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River"). The canal has one [lock](/wiki/Lock_(water_transport) "Lock (water transport)") near the downtown of Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu. In the winter, the city builds a [skating rink](/wiki/Ice_rink "Ice rink") on the canal near the lock. In the summer, the embankment on the east side of the canal has a 20-kilometre (12 mi) [cycling path](/wiki/Segregated_cycle_facilities "Segregated cycle facilities").

[![Plan of Fort Saint-Jean during the year 1750](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Fort_Saint-Jean_circa_1748.jpg/250px-Fort_Saint-Jean_circa_1748.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fort_Saint-Jean_circa_1748.jpg "Plan of Fort Saint-Jean during the year 1748")

Plan of Fort Saint-Jean during the year 1748

The French built [Fort Saint-Jean](/wiki/Fort_Saint-Jean_(Quebec) "Fort Saint-Jean (Quebec)") in the seventeenth century. Known to early English settlers as **St. Johns**, it provided an important communication link during the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"). During the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") control of the town [changed hands](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_St._Jean "Battle of Fort St. Jean") several times as British and American forces moved through the area.

In 2001 the city and several adjoining communities were merged into the new regional county municipality with a population to 79,600. This merger was requested by the five municipalities involved and was not part of the [municipal fusions](/wiki/Municipal_reorganization_in_Quebec "Municipal reorganization in Quebec") imposed by the Quebec government the following year.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Despite the fact that nearby Montreal is very racially diverse, in 2021 Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu had a very large majority of white residents (~94.4%). 4.1% of residents were visible minorities and 1.5% identified as Indigenous.[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17) The largest visible minority groups were [Black](/wiki/Black_Canadians "Black Canadians") (1.4%) and [Latin American](/wiki/Latin_American_Canadians "Latin American Canadians") (0.8%).

French was the mother tongue of 92.5% of residents. Other common mother tongues were English (2.5%), Spanish (0.8%), and Arabic (0.5%). 1.4% claimed both French and English as first languages, while 0.4% listed both French and a non-official language.

68.9% of residents were [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians"), down from 88.0% in 2011.[[18]](#cite_note-18) 62.3% were [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), 4.3% were Christian n.o.s and 0.8% were [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"). 29.3% of the population was non-religious or secular, up from 11.7% in 2011. All other religions and spiritual traditions accounted for 1.8% of the population. The largest non-Christian religion was [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") at 1.4%.

## Annual events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Annual events")]

A park hosts the annual [International Balloon Festival of Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu](/wiki/International_Balloon_Festival_of_Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu "International Balloon Festival of Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu"), a [hot air balloon festival](/wiki/Hot_air_balloon_festival "Hot air balloon festival") which attracts thousands of tourists.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Economy")]

Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu is home to the Carrefour Richelieu regional [shopping mall](/wiki/Shopping_mall "Shopping mall") which has 115 stores.[[19]](#cite_note-Westcliff-19)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

Newer retail developments include Faubourg Saint-Jean, home to restaurants, services, stores, and a soon-to-open movie theatre.

The historic downtown area, which borders the [Richelieu River](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River") and includes Richelieu and Champlain streets, is home to a variety of locally owned bars, restaurants, and shops.

St-Jean is a manufacturing centre for textiles, wood products, sporting equipment, and metal transformation. It hosts an Area Support Unit (ASU) of the [Canadian Armed Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Armed_Forces "Canadian Armed Forces"), which functions as a primary recruit and officer training establishment.

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

The Ville de [Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu public transit](/wiki/Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu_public_transit "Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu public transit") system provides commuter and local bus services.

According to the 2016 Census, 22,840 residents, or 56.7% of the labour force work within the city. An additional 5,135 (12.7%) commute to [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), while 2,305 (5.7%) work in [Longueuil](/wiki/Longueuil "Longueuil"), 1,440 (3.6%) work in [Brossard](/wiki/Brossard "Brossard"), and 965 (2.4%) work in [Chambly](/wiki/Chambly,_Quebec "Chambly, Quebec").

By contrast only 770 people commute from Montreal to work in Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu every day, while 795 people commute from [Longueuil](/wiki/Longueuil "Longueuil"), 780 commute from [Chambly](/wiki/Chambly,_Quebec "Chambly, Quebec"), 510 commute from [Saint-Alexandre](/wiki/Saint-Alexandre,_Quebec "Saint-Alexandre, Quebec") and 500 commute from [Mont-Saint-Grégoire](/wiki/Mont-Saint-Gr%C3%A9goire,_Quebec "Mont-Saint-Grégoire, Quebec").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Canal_de_Chambly.jpg/250px-Canal_de_Chambly.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canal_de_Chambly.jpg)

Chambly Canal

The city is split in two by Autoroute de la Vallée-des-Forts ([Autoroute 35](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_35 "Quebec Autoroute 35")) which goes north–south by going first through Saint-Luc district, then turns east just south of Pierre-Caisse Boulevard in Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu district to cross the Richelieu River and to finally continue its way south through St-Athanase and Iberville districts. The highway continues south for some 24 km before ending at Saint-Sébastien, but it is expected to be extended all the way to the [Canada-United States border](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border") at [Saint-Armand](/wiki/Saint-Armand,_Quebec "Saint-Armand, Quebec") ([Highgate Springs, Vermont](/wiki/Highgate_Springs,_Vermont "Highgate Springs, Vermont")) in the future, and will then continue as [Interstate 89](/wiki/Interstate_89 "Interstate 89") in Vermont.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu has its own municipal airport, [Saint-Jean Airport](/wiki/Saint-Jean_Airport "Saint-Jean Airport"), and is also close to [Montréal–Trudeau International Airport](/wiki/Montr%C3%A9al%E2%80%93Trudeau_International_Airport "Montréal–Trudeau International Airport").

The former [International Railway of Maine](/wiki/International_Railway_of_Maine "International Railway of Maine") runs through the town, now the connecting point for the [Central Maine and Quebec Railway](/wiki/Central_Maine_and_Quebec_Railway "Central Maine and Quebec Railway") with the [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway"). The former [Saint-Jean-d'Iberville railway station](/wiki/Saint-Jean-d%27Iberville_railway_station "Saint-Jean-d'Iberville railway station"), which until 1966 served the *[Ambassador](/wiki/Ambassador_(B%26M_train) "Ambassador (B&M train)")* to Boston and New York City and the *[Washingtonian](/wiki/Montrealer_(train)#Previous_service "Montrealer (train)")* to Washington, D.C., is now a preserved building.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Education")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu&action=edit&section=). *(November 2014)* |

The [South Shore Protestant Regional School Board](/wiki/South_Shore_Protestant_Regional_School_Board "South Shore Protestant Regional School Board") previously served the municipality.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In addition to more than a dozen public elementary and secondary schools, St-Jean is home to two private schools, one English-language school, and two higher education institutions:

* Ecole du quatre vent elementary French school
* École Vision Saint-Jean, a trilingual (French-English-Spanish) primary school
* École Secondaire Marcellin Champagnat, a historically Catholic (now non-religious) high school
* Saint-John's School, the city's only English-language school, which serves students from Kindergarten through high school. Per Quebec law, only children whose parents attended English-language school are allowed to attend English school themselves; French is mandatory for everyone else.
* [Royal Military College Saint-Jean](/wiki/Royal_Military_College_Saint-Jean "Royal Military College Saint-Jean") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Collège militaire royal de Saint-Jean*) serves as a one-year preparatory program for the [Royal Military College of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Military_College_of_Canada "Royal Military College of Canada") in [Kingston, Ontario](/wiki/Kingston,_Ontario "Kingston, Ontario"). Original founded in 1952, it ceased being a degree granting military college in 1995 due to cuts to military funding. RMCSJ continued to provide non-degree [college](/wiki/College_education_in_Quebec "College education in Quebec") programs for French-speaking cadets of the Canadian Forces. The Canadian federal government reopened the military college at Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu in the fall of 2007 to provide the full first year of university, equivalent to the Kingston program, for students with English- or French-language backgrounds alongside the college program.
* CEGEP Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu, part of Quebec's [CEGEP](/wiki/CEGEP "CEGEP") network, offering post-secondary, pre-university programs
